subject: ap-psychology
title: Personality, Motivation, and Social Psychology — Concept Application

parts.prompt: Explain how Spotlight effect relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Jordan overestimates how much the people at her new school  are paying attention to/judging her.

required_elements: Jordan feels so awkward her first few days because she thinks everyone is staring at her. Jordan believes that other students are noticing her, even when they are not.
alternatives.correct_example: Jordan feels so awkward her first few days because she thinks everyone is staring at her. Jordan believes that other students are noticing her, even when they are not.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must indicate that Jordan overestimates how much people at her new school are paying attention to or judging her. Responses that describe Jordan enjoying attention, or that describe generic worry without the specific mechanism of overestimating others' attention, do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Jordan feels so awkward her first few days because she thinks everyone is staring at her — overestimating how much attention others are actually paying to her.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies the spotlight effect: Jordan overestimates the degree to which others notice and judge her.

sample_responses.response: Jordan believes that other students are noticing her mistakes, even when they are not.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — captures the core of the spotlight effect (overestimating others' attention) applied to the new school context.

sample_responses.response: Jordan enjoys being in the spotlight and getting everyone's attention.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes enjoying attention, which is not the spotlight effect. The spotlight effect is about overestimating — not enjoying — others' scrutiny.

sample_responses.response: Jordan worries that people are staring at her.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague. Does not indicate that Jordan overestimates the actual degree of attention — mere worry without the overestimation component is insufficient.

parts.prompt: Explain how Cognitive map relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description:
Response must indicate that Jordan forms a mental representation of the school layout, which  allows her to navigate. 

OR 

Response must indicate that Jordan does not form a mental representation of the school  layout, which hinders her navigation.

required_elements: After a few weeks at the school, Jordan has developed a pretty good mental representation of the school's layout, so she can easily find everything now. Because Jordan never developed a mental representation of the layout of her new school, she struggles to find the gymnasium.
alternatives.correct_example: After a few weeks at the school, Jordan has developed a pretty good mental representation of the school's layout, so she can easily find everything now. Because Jordan never developed a mental representation of the layout of her new school, she struggles to find the gymnasium.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must explain that a cognitive map is a mental representation of a spatial layout, and apply it to Jordan's navigation of her new school. Either direction works: a well-developed cognitive map helps her navigate, or lacking one hinders navigation. Responses that describe Jordan consulting an external physical map rather than forming an internal mental representation do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: After a few weeks at the school, Jordan has developed a pretty good mental representation of the school's layout, so she can easily find her classrooms now.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly describes forming a cognitive map (mental representation of the school) that aids navigation.

sample_responses.response: Because Jordan never developed a mental representation of the layout of her new school, she struggles to find the gymnasium.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — shows how the absence of a cognitive map hinders spatial navigation in the new environment.

sample_responses.response: Jordan memorized the map of her school to get around.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes consulting an external physical map, not forming an internal mental/spatial representation. A cognitive map is a mental construct, not a physical document.

parts.prompt: Explain how Jordan's low level of the Big Five trait of extraversion relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Jordan's low level of extraversion (e.g., lack of sociability, lack of outgoingness, not getting energy from large crowds, etc.) will make her less likely to interact with others at school.

required_elements: Because Jordan is not that outgoing, she finds it hard to make friends at her school. Because Jordan is not comfortable interacting with others, she won't ask questions of the teacher.
alternatives.correct_example: Because Jordan is not that outgoing, she finds it hard to make friends at her school. Because Jordan is not comfortable interacting with others, she won't ask questions of the teacher.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must correctly apply LOW extraversion (not high) as an enduring trait that reduces Jordan's social interaction at her new school. Descriptors of low extraversion include: introverted, not outgoing, not sociable, not energized by large groups. Responses that describe high extraversion (outgoing, making friends easily) do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Because Jordan is not that outgoing (low extraversion), she finds it hard to initiate conversations and make friends at her new school.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies low extraversion as a lack of outgoingness that reduces social interaction.

sample_responses.response: Because Jordan is not comfortable interacting with others, she avoids participating in class discussions and asking the teacher questions.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — describes reduced social interaction in an academic setting as a consequence of low extraversion.

sample_responses.response: Jordan is outgoing so she makes friends easily.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes HIGH extraversion, which contradicts the prompt. The prompt specifies low extraversion.

parts.prompt: Jordan tries out for and makes the debate team. Jordan has made friends with other members of the team, and she really enjoys sitting with them at lunch every day. The coach is impressed with Jordan's debate skills, so he makes her captain of the team. When Jordan starts to help the coach plan for the next big debate, her friends stop sitting with her at lunch. She then stops helping the coach plan for the next debate but ends up winning it. Explain how Negative punishment relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that because Jordan's friends stop sitting with her at lunch,  she stops helping the coach plan for the next debate.

required_elements: Because Jordan's friends stop sitting with her at lunch, Jordan stops helping the coach plan the debate. Jordan stops planning with the coach because her friends stop sitting with her
alternatives.correct_example: Because Jordan's friends stop sitting with her at lunch, Jordan stops helping the coach plan the debate. Jordan stops planning with the coach because her friends stop sitting with her

scoring_points.official_rubric: Negative punishment = removing a desirable stimulus (friends sitting with her at lunch) which decreases a behavior (helping the coach plan). The response must show both: (1) something desirable was removed and (2) the behavior decreased as a result. Responses that describe bad things happening without the specific mechanism of removal-reducing-behavior do not earn the point. Responses that show Jordan strategically stopping to win back her friends (instrumentally) rather than as a conditioned response do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Because Jordan's friends stop sitting with her at lunch (removal of a desirable stimulus), Jordan stops helping the coach plan the debate — this is negative punishment decreasing that behavior.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the removal of a desirable stimulus (lunch companionship) and its consequence of reducing the target behavior (helping the coach).

sample_responses.response: Jordan stops planning with the coach because her friends stop sitting with her at lunch.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — clearly shows the contingency: removal of social reward caused the behavior to stop.

sample_responses.response: Jordan stops helping the coach to get her friends to sit with her at lunch again.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes a strategic, goal-directed behavior change rather than the automatic conditioning mechanism of negative punishment.

parts.prompt: Jordan tries out for and makes the debate team. Jordan has made friends with other members of the team, and she really enjoys sitting with them at lunch every day. The coach is impressed with Jordan's debate skills, so he makes her captain of the team. When Jordan starts to help the coach plan for the next big debate, her friends stop sitting with her at lunch. She then stops helping the coach plan for the next debate but ends up winning it. Explain how Formal operational stage of cognitive development relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Jordan is in the stage of development where she can  think abstractly and hypothetically (e.g., critical thinking, reasoning, etc.).

required_elements: Jordan being good at debate shows that she can think abstractly and hypothetically, which is formal operational thinking.
alternatives.correct_example: Jordan being good at debate shows that she can think abstractly and hypothetically, which is formal operational thinking.

wrong_examples: Jordan can think well now that she has grown older.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must identify the formal operational stage as the stage characterized by abstract and hypothetical thinking, and apply it to Jordan's debate skill (which requires abstract reasoning and hypothetical argumentation). Responses that describe general cognitive improvement with age without naming the specific capacities of the formal operational stage do not earn the point. References to sensorimotor, preoperational, or concrete operational thinking also do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Jordan being skilled at debate shows she can think abstractly and hypothetically — skills characteristic of the formal operational stage of cognitive development.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies abstract and hypothetical thinking as the hallmark of the formal operational stage and links it to debate skill.

sample_responses.response: In the formal operational stage, Jordan can reason about hypothetical arguments and consider abstract concepts, which makes her an effective debater.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — names the stage and its cognitive characteristics and applies them to the scenario.

sample_responses.response: Jordan can think well now that she has grown older.
sample_responses.commentary: References maturation without identifying the specific cognitive abilities (abstract/hypothetical reasoning) of the formal operational stage.

parts.prompt: Jordan tries out for and makes the debate team. Jordan has made friends with other members of the team, and she really enjoys sitting with them at lunch every day. The coach is impressed with Jordan's debate skills, so he makes her captain of the team. When Jordan starts to help the coach plan for the next big debate, her friends stop sitting with her at lunch. She then stops helping the coach plan for the next debate but ends up winning it. Explain how Actor-observer bias concerning Jordan's debate performance relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Jordan attributes her own behavior to situational factors AND that others in the scenario attribute that same behavior to dispositional factors.

required_elements: Jordan believes she won the debate because she had the time to prepare, but Jordan's teammates think she won because she is smart.
alternatives.correct_example: Jordan believes she won the debate because she had the time to prepare, but Jordan's teammates think she won because she is smart.

wrong_examples: When Jordan is debating, the other debaters' think she acts fake.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Actor-observer bias requires BOTH components: (1) the actor (Jordan) attributes her own behavior to situational/external factors, AND (2) observers attribute the same behavior to dispositional/internal factors. A response that gives only one side does not earn the point. Responses that show observers making unrelated social judgments (e.g., acting fake) do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Jordan believes she won the debate because she had the time to prepare (situational attribution), but her teammates think she won because she is naturally intelligent (dispositional attribution).
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly demonstrates both halves of actor-observer bias: Jordan attributes her win to external circumstances; observers attribute it to her internal traits.

sample_responses.response: Jordan thinks she debated well because the topic was in her favor, but the coach believes she won because she is a talented debater.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — Jordan cites a situational factor (favorable topic) while the coach cites a dispositional factor (talent).

sample_responses.response: Jordan believes she is good at debate because she is intelligent.
sample_responses.commentary: Shows Jordan making a dispositional attribution about herself — the opposite of what actor-observer bias predicts. The actor should attribute to situational, not dispositional, factors.

parts.prompt: Jordan tries out for and makes the debate team. Jordan has made friends with other members of the team, and she really enjoys sitting with them at lunch every day. The coach is impressed with Jordan's debate skills, so he makes her captain of the team. When Jordan starts to help the coach plan for the next big debate, her friends stop sitting with her at lunch. She then stops helping the coach plan for the next debate but ends up winning it. Explain how Jordan's high level of self-efficacy relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Jordan believes she has the capability to accomplish a specific  task related to the scenario.

required_elements: Jordan believes that she can win her debate. Jordan is sure she can do what it takes to make friends.
alternatives.correct_example: Jordan believes that she can win her debate. Jordan is sure she can do what it takes to make friends.

scoring_points.official_rubric: Self-efficacy is the belief in one's capability to succeed at a specific task — not general confidence or self-esteem. The response must identify a specific task (winning the debate, making friends, handling the social situation) and show Jordan believing she can accomplish it. Responses that describe self-confidence generally without tying it to a specific task do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Jordan believes she can win the debate even though she stopped planning with the coach — her high self-efficacy means she is confident in her debate abilities for this specific task.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies a specific task (winning the debate) and connects it to Jordan's belief in her own capability.

sample_responses.response: Jordan is sure she can do what it takes to make friends at her new school.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — names a specific task (making friends) and links it to Jordan's belief in her capability to accomplish it.

sample_responses.response: Jordan has high self-efficacy, so she believes in herself.
sample_responses.commentary: Too generic. Does not identify a specific task that Jordan believes she can accomplish. Self-efficacy is always task-specific, not a general trait.

sample_responses.response: Jordan thinks she is smart.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes a self-assessment of general ability, not a belief in capability for a specific task. This is closer to self-esteem than self-efficacy.
